theorem: Let A, B be invertible n×n matrices. Prove that AB is invertible and (AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A and B are invertible n×n matrices.
GOAL: N is the inverse of M iff MN = NM = I. To prove (AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}, it suffices to show (AB)(B^{-1}A^{-1}) = I and (B^{-1}A^{-1})(AB) = I.
STEP 1: (AB)(B^{-1}A^{-1}) = A(BB^{-1})A^{-1} = A·I·A^{-1} = I, by associativity and BB^{-1} = I.
STEP 2: (B^{-1}A^{-1})(AB) = B^{-1}(A^{-1}A)B = B^{-1}·I·B = I, by associativity and A^{-1}A = I.
CONCLUSION: B^{-1}A^{-1} is a two-sided inverse of AB, so AB is invertible and (AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}. ∎

theorem: Prove that the inverse of an invertible matrix A is unique.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an n×n matrix and B, C are both inverses of A.
GOAL: N is the inverse of M iff MN = NM = I. To prove uniqueness, it suffices to show any two inverses B and C of A satisfy B = C.
STEP 1: Since B is an inverse, AB = BA = I; since C is an inverse, AC = CA = I.
STEP 2: B = B·I = B(AC) = (BA)C = I·C = C, using associativity and the inverse identities.
CONCLUSION: Any two inverses of A are equal, so the inverse of A is unique. ∎

theorem: Prove that if A is invertible, then A^{-1} is invertible and (A^{-1})^{-1} = A.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an invertible n×n matrix.
GOAL: N is the inverse of M iff MN = NM = I. To prove (A^{-1})^{-1} = A, it suffices to show A^{-1}·A = I and A·A^{-1} = I.
STEP 1: Since A is invertible, by definition A A^{-1} = I and A^{-1} A = I.
STEP 2: These two equations say exactly that A is a two-sided inverse of A^{-1}.
CONCLUSION: A^{-1} is invertible and its inverse is A, so (A^{-1})^{-1} = A. ∎

theorem: Prove that a square matrix with a row of zeros is not invertible.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an n×n matrix whose i-th row is entirely zero.
GOAL: A is invertible iff there is a B with AB = BA = I. To prove A is NOT invertible, it suffices to show no B satisfies AB = I (A has no right inverse).
STEP 1: For any n×n matrix B, row i of the product AB equals (row i of A)·B = 0·B = 0, so row i of AB is all zeros.
STEP 2: The identity matrix I has a 1 in position (i,i), so its i-th row is not zero; hence AB ≠ I for every B.
CONCLUSION: No B satisfies AB = I, so A has no right inverse and is therefore not invertible. ∎

theorem: Prove that a square matrix with a column of zeros is not invertible.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an n×n matrix whose j-th column is entirely zero.
GOAL: A is invertible iff there is a B with AB = BA = I. To prove A is NOT invertible, it suffices to show no B satisfies BA = I (A has no left inverse).
STEP 1: For any n×n matrix B, column j of the product BA equals B·(column j of A) = B·0 = 0, so column j of BA is all zeros.
STEP 2: The identity matrix I has a 1 in position (j,j), so its j-th column is not zero; hence BA ≠ I for every B.
CONCLUSION: No B satisfies BA = I, so A has no left inverse and is therefore not invertible. ∎

theorem: Prove that the null space of an m×n matrix A is a subspace of R^n.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an m×n matrix and Null(A) = { x in R^n : Ax = 0 }.
GOAL: A subset W of R^n is a subspace iff (i) 0 is in W, (ii) W is closed under addition, (iii) W is closed under scalar multiplication. We verify all three for Null(A).
STEP 1 (zero vector): A·0 = 0, so 0 is in Null(A).
STEP 2 (closed under addition): If x, y are in Null(A) then Ax = 0 and Ay = 0, so A(x+y) = Ax + Ay = 0; thus x+y is in Null(A).
STEP 3 (closed under scalar multiplication): If x is in Null(A) and c is a scalar, then A(cx) = c(Ax) = c·0 = 0; thus cx is in Null(A).
CONCLUSION: All three conditions hold, so Null(A) is a subspace of R^n. ∎

theorem: Prove that the column space of an m×n matrix A is a subspace of R^m.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an m×n matrix and Col(A) = { Ax : x in R^n }.
GOAL: A subset W of R^m is a subspace iff (i) 0 is in W, (ii) W is closed under addition, (iii) W is closed under scalar multiplication. We verify all three for Col(A).
STEP 1 (zero vector): A·0 = 0, so 0 = A·0 is in Col(A).
STEP 2 (closed under addition): If u = Ax and v = Ay are in Col(A), then u + v = Ax + Ay = A(x+y), which is in Col(A).
STEP 3 (closed under scalar multiplication): If u = Ax is in Col(A) and c is a scalar, then cu = c(Ax) = A(cx), which is in Col(A).
CONCLUSION: All three conditions hold, so Col(A) is a subspace of R^m. ∎

theorem: Prove that the set of solutions of a homogeneous system Ax = 0 is a subspace.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an m×n matrix and S = { x in R^n : Ax = 0 } is the solution set of the homogeneous system.
GOAL: A subset W of R^n is a subspace iff (i) 0 is in W, (ii) W is closed under addition, (iii) W is closed under scalar multiplication. We verify all three for S.
STEP 1 (zero vector): A·0 = 0, so 0 is in S.
STEP 2 (closed under addition): If x, y are in S then Ax = 0 and Ay = 0, so A(x+y) = Ax + Ay = 0; thus x+y is in S.
STEP 3 (closed under scalar multiplication): If x is in S and c is a scalar, then A(cx) = c(Ax) = 0; thus cx is in S.
CONCLUSION: All three conditions hold, so the solution set of Ax = 0 is a subspace. ∎

theorem: Prove that if a set of nonzero vectors is orthogonal, then it is linearly independent.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: v_1, ..., v_k are nonzero vectors that are pairwise orthogonal, so v_i · v_j = 0 for i ≠ j.
GOAL: A set is linearly independent iff the only solution of c_1 v_1 + ... + c_k v_k = 0 is c_1 = ... = c_k = 0. We show every coefficient must be zero.
STEP 1: Suppose c_1 v_1 + ... + c_k v_k = 0. Fix an index i and take the dot product of both sides with v_i.
STEP 2: The left side becomes sum_j c_j (v_j · v_i) = c_i (v_i · v_i), since v_j · v_i = 0 for j ≠ i; the right side is 0. So c_i (v_i · v_i) = 0.
STEP 3: Because v_i is nonzero, v_i · v_i > 0, so c_i = 0. As i was arbitrary, every coefficient is zero.
CONCLUSION: The only linear combination equal to 0 is the trivial one, so the set is linearly independent. ∎

theorem: Prove that any subset of a linearly independent set is linearly independent.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: S = { v_1, ..., v_k } is linearly independent and T is a subset of S, say T = { v_1, ..., v_m } with m ≤ k.
GOAL: A set is linearly independent iff the only solution of a linear combination equal to 0 is the trivial one. We show this holds for T.
STEP 1: Suppose c_1 v_1 + ... + c_m v_m = 0. Extend it to a combination over all of S by setting the remaining coefficients c_{m+1} = ... = c_k = 0.
STEP 2: Then c_1 v_1 + ... + c_k v_k = 0 is a linear dependence among the vectors of S.
STEP 3: Since S is linearly independent, all coefficients are zero; in particular c_1 = ... = c_m = 0.
CONCLUSION: The only combination of T equal to 0 is trivial, so T is linearly independent. ∎

theorem: Prove that a set containing the zero vector is linearly dependent.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: S = { v_1, ..., v_k } contains the zero vector; say v_1 = 0.
GOAL: A set is linearly dependent iff there is a nontrivial linear combination equal to 0 (some coefficient nonzero). We exhibit one.
STEP 1: Consider the combination 1·v_1 + 0·v_2 + ... + 0·v_k.
STEP 2: This equals 1·0 + 0 + ... + 0 = 0, yet the coefficient of v_1 is 1, which is nonzero.
CONCLUSION: There is a nontrivial combination equal to 0, so S is linearly dependent. ∎

theorem: Prove that a linear map T satisfies T(0) = 0.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: T : V → W is linear, meaning T(x+y) = T(x)+T(y) and T(cx) = cT(x) for all vectors x, y and scalars c.
GOAL: To prove T(0) = 0, use the scalar-multiplication property of linearity with the scalar 0.
STEP 1: By homogeneity, T(0) = T(0·0) = 0·T(0).
STEP 2: For any vector w, the scalar 0 times w is the zero vector, so 0·T(0) = 0.
CONCLUSION: Therefore T(0) = 0. ∎

theorem: Prove that the composition of two linear maps is linear.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: T : U → V and S : V → W are linear maps; let R = S∘T.
GOAL: A map is linear iff it satisfies additivity R(x+y) = R(x)+R(y) and homogeneity R(cx) = cR(x). We verify both for R.
STEP 1 (additivity): R(x+y) = S(T(x+y)) = S(T(x)+T(y)) = S(T(x)) + S(T(y)) = R(x) + R(y), using linearity of T then of S.
STEP 2 (homogeneity): R(cx) = S(T(cx)) = S(cT(x)) = cS(T(x)) = cR(x), using linearity of T then of S.
CONCLUSION: R = S∘T satisfies both properties, so the composition of linear maps is linear. ∎

theorem: Prove that the map T(x) = Ax given by an m×n matrix A is linear.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an m×n matrix and T : R^n → R^m is defined by T(x) = Ax.
GOAL: A map is linear iff it satisfies additivity T(x+y) = T(x)+T(y) and homogeneity T(cx) = cT(x). We verify both.
STEP 1 (additivity): T(x+y) = A(x+y) = Ax + Ay = T(x) + T(y), by the distributive law of matrix multiplication.
STEP 2 (homogeneity): T(cx) = A(cx) = c(Ax) = cT(x), since scalars commute with matrix multiplication.
CONCLUSION: T satisfies both properties, so T(x) = Ax is linear. ∎

theorem: Prove that if T : V → W is linear, then T(-x) = -T(x).
proof:
ASSUMPTION: T : V → W is linear, so T(cx) = cT(x) for every scalar c and vector x.
GOAL: To prove T(-x) = -T(x), apply homogeneity with the scalar c = -1.
STEP 1: Since -x = (-1)x, homogeneity gives T(-x) = T((-1)x) = (-1)T(x).
STEP 2: By definition (-1)T(x) = -T(x).
CONCLUSION: Therefore T(-x) = -T(x). ∎

theorem: Prove that a linear map T : V → W is injective iff its kernel is {0}.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: T : V → W is linear and ker(T) = { x in V : T(x) = 0 }.
GOAL: This is an 'iff', so prove both directions: (⇒) if T is injective then ker(T) = {0}; (⇐) if ker(T) = {0} then T is injective.
STEP 1 (⇒): Suppose T is injective. If x is in ker(T) then T(x) = 0 = T(0), and injectivity forces x = 0, so ker(T) = {0}.
STEP 2 (⇐): Suppose ker(T) = {0}. If T(x) = T(y) then T(x-y) = T(x) - T(y) = 0, so x-y is in ker(T), giving x-y = 0, i.e. x = y; hence T is injective.
CONCLUSION: Both directions hold, so T is injective iff ker(T) = {0}. ∎

theorem: Prove that (A^T)^T = A for any matrix A.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an m×n matrix, and the transpose is defined by (A^T)_{ij} = A_{ji}.
GOAL: Two matrices are equal iff they have the same size and equal entries in every position. We check both for (A^T)^T and A.
STEP 1 (sizes): A^T is n×m, so (A^T)^T is m×n, the same size as A.
STEP 2 (entries): ((A^T)^T)_{ij} = (A^T)_{ji} = A_{ij} for every i, j, by applying the transpose definition twice.
CONCLUSION: The two matrices have the same size and identical entries, so (A^T)^T = A. ∎

theorem: Prove that (AB)^T = B^T A^T for matrices A (m×n) and B (n×p).
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is m×n and B is n×p, so AB is m×p and (AB)^T is p×m; transpose satisfies (M^T)_{ij} = M_{ji}.
GOAL: Two matrices are equal iff they have the same size and equal entries everywhere. We check both for (AB)^T and B^T A^T.
STEP 1 (sizes): B^T is p×n and A^T is n×m, so B^T A^T is p×m, matching the size of (AB)^T.
STEP 2 (entries): ((AB)^T)_{ij} = (AB)_{ji} = sum_k A_{jk} B_{ki} = sum_k (B^T)_{ik} (A^T)_{kj} = (B^T A^T)_{ij}, for all i, j.
CONCLUSION: The two matrices have the same size and equal entries, so (AB)^T = B^T A^T. ∎

theorem: Prove that if A is invertible then A^T is invertible and (A^T)^{-1} = (A^{-1})^T.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: A is an invertible n×n matrix, so A A^{-1} = A^{-1} A = I.
GOAL: N is the inverse of M iff MN = NM = I. To prove (A^T)^{-1} = (A^{-1})^T, it suffices to show A^T (A^{-1})^T = I and (A^{-1})^T A^T = I.
STEP 1: A^T (A^{-1})^T = (A^{-1} A)^T = I^T = I, using the transpose-of-product rule and A^{-1}A = I.
STEP 2: (A^{-1})^T A^T = (A A^{-1})^T = I^T = I, using the transpose-of-product rule and A A^{-1} = I.
CONCLUSION: (A^{-1})^T is a two-sided inverse of A^T, so A^T is invertible and (A^T)^{-1} = (A^{-1})^T. ∎

theorem: Prove that if v_1, ..., v_n span a vector space V, then every vector in V has at least one representation as a linear combination of them.
proof:
ASSUMPTION: v_1, ..., v_n span V, meaning span{v_1, ..., v_n} = V.
GOAL: By definition, span{v_1, ..., v_n} is the set of all linear combinations of v_1, ..., v_n. To prove the claim, show every w in V lies in this set.
STEP 1: Let w be any vector in V. Since the vectors span V, we have V = span{v_1, ..., v_n}, so w is an element of span{v_1, ..., v_n}.
STEP 2: By the definition of span, membership means w = c_1 v_1 + ... + c_n v_n for some scalars c_1, ..., c_n.
CONCLUSION: Every w in V can be written as a linear combination of v_1, ..., v_n. ∎
